title: Dichotomous electronic system in a bilayer Ni$^{1+}$ nickelate
authors: Young-Joon Song, W. E. Pickett, K. -W. Lee
abstract: ``Infinite layer'' nickelates (ILNs) ${\cal R}$NiO$_2$ (${\cal R}$=rare earth elements), having empty apical O sites, become superconducting upon hole doping. They display a secondary electron Fermi surface (FS), giving hole doping, arising not from atomic orbitals but from a band based on interstitial density. Newly reported La$_3$Ni$_2$O$_5$F, formally Ni$^{1+}$, provides an unexpected example of ILN with essentially ideal two dimensional character. A partially occupied single band $E^*$, based on interstitial density, has distinct properties, as its strongly anisotropic shape extends over the three ``apical'' layers and leads to a cylindrical electron FS giving self-doping. This interstitial density is associated with a {\it network of valence bands}, including a Ni $d_{xz},d_{yz}$ pair that partners with $E^*$ to provide an incipient non-analytic Dirac point, leading to an unusual type of interstitial density--$d$ band coupling. The $E^*$ electron band and the conventional Ni $dpσ$ band will display a dichotomy of hole and electron quasiparticle behavior in normal state transport and far-IR properties, and likely resulting in unconventional superconducting state properties even for nickelates.
summary: The paper investigates the electronic properties of $	ext{Ni}^{1+}$ nickelates, focusing on how an interstitial density ($E^*$) band creates a second set of charge carriers alongside conventional $d p\sigma$ electrons. The authors argue that this 'dichotomous' carrier behavior leads to unique transport signatures and suggests unconventional pairing mechanisms for superconductivity.
main_problem: The study investigates the electronic structure and potential superconducting mechanisms in novel infinite layer nickelates ($	ext{ILNs}$) like $	ext{La}_3	ext{Ni}_2	ext{O}_5	ext{F}$. The core challenge is understanding how two distinct carrier types—conventional $d p\sigma$ electrons and interstitial density ($E^*$) carriers—interact.
main_result: The system exhibits a dichotomous electronic structure, featuring both $	ext{dp}\sigma$ and $E^*$ Fermi surfaces. This interplay suggests unconventional superconducting properties arising from the coupling between these two distinct carrier channels.
method: Density functional theory calculations (using wien2k) are employed to map out band structures, orbital character, and electronic density distributions across different high-symmetry points.
model_or_system: The physical system is a bilayer $	ext{Ni}^{1+}$ nickelate ($	ext{La}_3	ext{Ni}_2	ext{O}_5	ext{F}$), which possesses an interstitial density ($E^*$) band alongside the conventional Ni $d p\sigma$ bands. The structure is analyzed for its quasi-two-dimensional character.
key_observables: Fermi surface topology (cylindrical electron FS from $E^*$ vs. hole FS from $	ext{dp}\sigma$), orbital splitting ($	ext{dz}^2$ orbitals), transport coefficients ($\sigma, R_H$), and the approach to Dirac degeneracy points.
important_parameters: Doping level (hole doping via $E^*$), strain (compressive strain), and anion composition (tuning F concentration).
main_assumptions: The analysis often assumes 2D transport for simplicity in calculating conductivity, and the superconducting state is hypothesized based on observed electronic features.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the tetragonal structure and isosurfaces of the $E^*$ wavefunction. Band structures show orbital splitting due to interstitial coupling, and plots detail the evolution towards band degeneracies at high-symmetry points.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by establishing the unique electronic nature of $	ext{La}_3	ext{Ni}_2	ext{O}_5	ext{F}$ via its $E^*$ band. It then analyzes the interplay between the two carrier types, predicting distinct transport signatures and suggesting implications for unconventional superconductivity.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant as it tackles strongly correlated materials (nickelates) using advanced electronic structure calculations to predict novel superconducting mechanisms based on orbital/band dichotomy, which is a key theme in modern condensed matter theory.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Left: tetragonal I4/mmm structure of La3Ni2O5F, consisting of double NiO2 infinite layers isolated by the La2[X]La2 layer. Right: isosurface plot of the occupied E∗

FIG. 2. Nonmagnetic band structure of La3Ni2O5F. (a) Ni 3d orbital decompositions are distinguished by color. The blue arrows indicate the large splitting of the dz2 orbitals at the Γ point due to coupling with, or through, the interstitial (I) density in the intervening La1 layer. Points are: except for the usual dpσ band, the other Ni d bands lie at -1.3± 0.5 eV, hence are electrically inactive; t2g splitting is small. (b) fatband representation of the I character, that is, the amplitude of the state not falling within any atomic sphere. The E∗band dips down from 2 eV at Γ to −0.5 eV at M. See the text for other I density related features.

FIG. 3. Top row: isosurface plots of wave functions |ψ(r)|: the even (a) and odd (b) dz2 pair at the M point, at −2.4 eV and −1.5 eV respectively, and (c) the E∗state at −0.5 eV. Bottom row: (d)-(f), corresponding 2D-projected color-scale plots on the [110] plane, passing through La and Ni atoms, making the z reflection symmetry clear, as well as the involvement of I density. Fig. 2 shows that the dz2 pair can be followed throughout the zone, with splitting of ∼1 eV. The D∗state at M, part of the network as described in the text, is shown in SM.

FIG. 4. Evolutions of the E∗and D∗bands, meeting and crossing, enlarged from the corresponding region of Fig. 1. Top row: increasing the F concentration through the critical point at F(0.700). Note the switching of eigenvalue order at F(0.705). Bottom row: applying symmetric strain, with a critical point at ∆a=−1.84%. The lattice parameter a is changed as noted, with fixed volume. Decreasing a could be achieved by pressure, since the change in c may be have negligible effect. In each case, the upper E∗band shifts downward at M, meeting the D∗band.
